final_answer:
No, not in the current code: there is no built-in “after some time automatically become permissionless” mechanism.

What this code does today is a permissioned validator flow. A new validator must first be explicitly approved by the configured approval authority, then submit a join transaction that exactly matches that approval (operator, signer key, epoch, nonce, and stake limits). If that approval does not exist, join is rejected. Consensus finality is handled by CometBFT, and validator set updates happen through the stake module end-block logic. So validators are not “open-join” today. Also, `GOLD` delegation in this code affects reward weighting, not validator admission.

Why this matters for the Gold Chain objective is simple: you cannot assume future permissionless behavior from this code path. Right now, control of validator entry is an explicit governance/security choice.

What should be done next is to decide explicitly whether you want to stay permissioned or migrate. If you want migration, the concrete path is: define a governance-approved transition height, add a new admission mode in code that removes mandatory pre-approval, enforce objective on-chain eligibility checks (minimum self-stake, signer uniqueness, slashing/jailing compatibility, power caps, anti-spam controls), and ship it as a planned upgrade with full migration tests. Without those code changes, it stays permissioned.
prompt: Ok I understand none of this. Could you explain to me properly not in summary bullet points but also is there a method or mechanism whereby after a while it can become permissionless?
